turns.content: Imagine I'm building a large library with ten crates and I want to enable LTO so that the Rust compiler can inline functions across all these modules. My assumption is that setting lto = true in my Cargo.toml will just turn on aggressive optimizations everywhere.

That's a common but slightly off assumption for beginners. Setting lto = true does enable full Link-Time Optimization, which allows the Rust compiler to inline functions and merge code across all crates during the link phase, not just compile them individually. However, it often leads to significantly longer build times because the entire dependency graph gets re-analyzed every time you change a single function.

I see, so I'm paying a heavy price in compilation speed just for better code generation. But what if I only want some inter-crate optimizations without the massive slowdown? Is there a lighter option?

Exactly! That's where lto = "thin" comes in as the idiomatic solution for most projects. While lto = true performs full LTO which can take minutes or even hours on large projects, lto = "thin" enables partial optimizations like cross-crate inlining without the heavy cost of merging all code at once. It keeps build times reasonable while still capturing some of the benefits you're looking for.

Got it, so thin LTO is a middle ground between nothing and full on. But I'm worried about binary size—won't turning off that aggressive merging mean my final executable grows larger than if I had just used lto = true?

You're right to be concerned, but the trade-off isn't always linear. Thin LTO often produces binaries slightly smaller or comparable to no optimization at all because it skips some of the complex merging steps that can actually bloat the code if not managed carefully. Full LTO sometimes shrinks binaries more aggressively due to better data layout and dead code elimination, but thin LTO is usually sufficient for most applications where you need a balance between compile time and binary size.
Okay, so if I'm doing a personal project with maybe three or four crates, should I just stick with lto = "thin"? Or is there a specific case where I'd really need the full thing?

For a personal project like that, lto = "thin" is almost certainly the right choice. You'll get cross-crate inlining without burning CPU cycles on massive dependency graph scans. The only time you'd genuinely need full LTO with lto = true is if you're building a production-grade system library where every byte counts and your team can afford to wait for longer build times during development.

I'm trying to speed up my build pipeline like I do with CMake, but I keep reading that Rust LTO is a game-changer. It feels similar to how gcc's -flto flag fuses functions across object files, and I thought enabling it in Cargo.toml would just make everything compile faster by default.

That analogy with gcc is good for the concept of cross-file optimization, but there's a misconception here: Rust's LTO isn't an on/off switch that magically improves all builds. Enabling lto = true in Cargo.toml forces full link-time optimization which significantly increases compile time and can break incremental compiles because the compiler must re-evaluate everything after each change.

I see, so I was assuming that turning on lto = true would just be like adding -flto to gcc. Does it really hurt performance? I thought Rust's optimizer is smart enough to handle this without breaking my workflow.

You're right about the optimizer being smart, but not for incremental builds. When you enable lto = true, the compiler performs aggressive cross-crate inlining during the link phase, which means it re-optimizes shared code between all crates in your workspace every time you build. This defeats the purpose of fast iteration because the build becomes non-incremental.
Okay, so I need to avoid lto = true for daily development? But what about thin LTO? Is that just a middle ground that lets me get some optimization without the full cost?

Yes, exactly. Setting lto = "thin" in Cargo.toml enables link-time optimizations only at the end of the build process and skips the expensive cross-crate inlining during compilation. This gives you some codegen benefits like improved binary size without breaking incremental builds.
That sounds much better for my workflow, but I'm still worried about binary size. Does thin LTO actually shrink the final executable compared to not using it at all?

Thin LTO does reduce binary size by performing optimizations like dead code elimination and constant propagation at link time, but the savings are usually marginal for most projects. The trade-off is that you gain some optimization benefits while keeping fast incremental compiles, which fits most development scenarios.
So my strategy should be to use lto = "thin" in Cargo.toml and only enable full LTO for release builds where speed isn't the bottleneck?

That's a solid approach. For most projects, thin LTO provides the best balance of build speed and binary efficiency. If you need maximum optimization for production binaries, you can enable lto = true in your release profile, but remember to rebuild from scratch since incremental builds won't work.
